Your sellers researched an account last Tuesday. Something changed on Thursday. A new technology deployment. A leadership shift. A contract renewal window opening.

Nobody noticed. The account went cold!

This is the problem with point-in-time research. It decays the moment it's done. And in partner selling — where you're managing hundreds of accounts across vendor product lines — manually re-researching isn't a workflow. It’s a fantasy.

That’s why we built Custom Research Agents to be always-on.

Here’s how it works:
* Define the datapoint you care about — competitive tech stack, hiring patterns, or compliance posture.
* Enlyft researches it across your target accounts.
* The agent keeps watching. Continuously.
* Get notified in Slack or Teams the moment something significant changes.

No daily logins. No "let me check on that account." No stale briefs sitting in a CRM field from three weeks ago. Your research stays current because the agent never stops running.

The shift here is subtle but important: research isn’t a task anymore. It’s a signal. An always-on signal tuned to exactly what your sellers need to know — delivered where they already work. 
️

We shipped this a few weeks ago. The early results are clear: sellers are catching account changes they would have missed entirely, and spending their time on outreach instead of Google tabs.

Always-on research isn't a feature. It's the new default.

Most procurement teams aren’t behind because they lack talent.

They’re behind because the system keeps them permanently on fire.

Here’s what that actually looks like:

An invoice lands without a PO.

Someone chases the supplier.

The supplier sends a corrected version.

Finance flags it anyway.

It gets escalated.

Three people have a meeting about it.

That’s a Tuesday.

And while your procurement team is knee-deep in that chaos — a contract quietly auto-renews at last year’s pricing. 

A new supplier with better terms goes unevaluated. 

A cost-saving opportunity your CEO asked about last quarter? 

Still “in progress.”

Reactive procurement doesn’t happen because people are lazy.

It happens because operational noise crowds out strategic thought.

When your team spends 70% of their time on data entry, chasing approvals, and fixing mismatches — the remaining 30% isn’t enough for the work that actually moves the business forward.

Supplier relationship development. 
Spend analysis. 
Category strategy. 
Risk mapping.

These aren’t nice-to-haves. 

They’re the entire point of having a procurement function.

But you can’t think strategically when you’re always putting out fires.

The shift from reactive to proactive procurement isn’t about hiring more people.

It’s about removing the operational drag that’s stealing your team’s capacity.

When invoice matching is automated → your team stops chasing paperwork.

When PO validation runs in real time → exceptions surface before they become problems.

When vendor data is centralised and clean → decisions get made with confidence, not gut feel.

The team doesn’t change. The time available to them does.

Procurement leaders who’ve made this shift all say the same thing:

“I didn’t realise how much time we were losing until we stopped losing it.”

If your procurement team is talented but constantly reactive — the problem isn’t them.

It’s the system they’re working in.

Fix the system. Get your strategy back.

The five levers that actually scale an agency (and why order is everything)

Most agency founders know they’ve hit a ceiling.
 Revenue is up, but it feels harder than it should. More clients, more team, yet the founder is still the bottleneck and margins aren’t climbing with the top line.

An agency is a production business. Its entire value comes from one thing: turning a customer contract into delivered output at a positive margin… and doing that reliably at higher volume.

There are exactly five levers that control this:

1- Lead Volume
2- Sales Conversion
3- Delivery Repeatability
4- Churn Control
5- Hiring & Ramp Scalability

The levers work best when you pull them in that exact order.

You need to be able to reliably generate more leads. Which can be challenging, even for a marketing agency. 
 But you can’t convert what you don’t have.
 You can’t prove repeatable delivery without consistent customers walking in the door.
 You can’t control churn if the delivery isn’t repeatable.
 And you definitely can’t hire and ramp new people (or AI) without the first four already working.

Pull them out of order and you get the dangerous pattern most founders live in: revenue keeps climbing while contribution per customer actually falls. 

It looks like growth on the surface. It’s value destruction underneath.

Solve them in the right sequence and each new customer compounds instead of subtracting. The machine starts running without you in every decision. That’s when real scale becomes possible.

The agencies that break through $1M–$3M and keep their margins aren’t luckier or smarter.
 They simply stopped trying to fix everything at once and started fixing the next lever in line.

I am excited to share that I have started an internship with Enabled Talent, a Toronto-based HR tech innovator on a mission to bridge the global disability employment gap.

With over 1.3 billion people living with disabilities globally, the "Purple Pound" represents a $1.9 trillion economic opportunity. Yet, legacy recruitment systems and algorithmic biases continue to create systemic barriers.

As part of this role, I am working on a strategic roadmap focusing on three key pillars:

📊 Market Sizing (TAM, SAM, SOM): Quantifying the global shift toward inclusive hiring and identifying the immediate opportunities within federally regulated sectors. 
💰 Sustainable Pricing Models: Developing a tripartite revenue strategy—Employer Subscriptions, Enterprise Contracts, and Public-Sector Partnerships—to transition from a project-based model to a scalable global platform. 
🤖 AI-Driven Inclusion: Leveraging TARA, a proprietary AI voice companion, to turn accessibility into a competitive advantage for employers.

The goal is clear: ensure that "inclusion" is not just a policy, but a measurable, financially sustainable, and automated reality for organisations worldwide. 🌍♿️

I’m grateful to Amandipp S., CEO of Enabled Talent, for the opportunity to contribute to such a vital mission. A special thank you also to my tutor, Dr Bettina Coleman-Schoels, for facilitating this incredible experience and for her guidance in connecting academic theory with high-impact consultancy practice.

The New Global Side-Hustle: Training Your Future Robot Butler 🤖🏠

While the world watches polished videos of humanoid robots doing backflips, a massive, invisible labor market is working behind the scenes to teach them something much harder: How to fold a shirt.

Robotics leaders like Tesla, Figure AI, and Agility Robotics have realized that simulations aren't enough. Physics is "messy," and robots can’t learn the nuance of household chores from a line of code. They need to see us do it.

❗ The Rise of the "Human Movement" Economy:

The Gig: Thousands of contract workers across 50+ countries are now earning $15/hour by filming themselves performing everyday tasks—cooking, cleaning, and folding laundry—with iPhones strapped to their foreheads.

The Scale: In 2025 alone, $6 billion was invested in humanoid robotics. Companies are now spending $100M+ annually just to buy real-world movement data.

The Gatekeeper: This isn't your typical hiring process. AI agents (like Micro1’s "Zara") are now vetting, interviewing, and managing quality control for this global workforce at scale.

The Strange Loop 🔄 

Just as ChatGPT learned from our words, the next generation of robots is learning from our movements. We are currently seeing a new global labor market where people are earning a living teaching robots the very tasks those machines may eventually automate.

Is this the ultimate "work from home" evolution, or a sign of how data-hungry the robotics race has truly become?

The demand for human movement data is growing faster than anyone expected. Would you "rent" your daily chores to train an AI?

How Small Businesses Can Compete in a Skills-Based Labor Market
In our March 5, 2026, post, we discussed why hiring continues to feel harder than it should. The impact goes beyond recruiting. For many small businesses, labor constraints are shaping growth decisions.

When the right skills are not in place, expansion slows. Owners  hesitate to take on new contracts. They delay hiring. They stretch  current teams to cover gaps. Over time, that strain limits momentum.

Research from the Wharton School, in collaboration with Accenture,  indicates that the labor market has shifted from focusing on job titles  to emphasizing skills. The key question is not how many people you have;  rather, it is how many people you have. It is whether your team has the  capabilities required to execute consistently.
Start With the Work, Not the Title
Growth requires clarity. Break roles into the tasks that truly drive  outcomes. What must get done well every week? Where does work slow down?  Those answers reveal which skills matter most.
Look Inside Before Hiring Outside
Some of the capabilities you need may already exist within your team,  but are underdeveloped. A focused shift in responsibilities or targeted  retraining can strengthen execution faster than competing for scarce  talent in the open market.
Align Pay With What Drives Results
Leadership and communication matter, but they are widely available.  Operational depth, technical competence, and compliance awareness are  harder to find. Compensation should reflect the skills that reduce risk  and support delivery.
Use Technology With Intention
Technology, including AI, should simplify routine work and free your  team to focus on judgment and coordination. The goal is not to replace  people. It is to strengthen execution without adding headcount.
Mitigating labor constraints is not just about filling roles. It is about building the capacity to grow with confidence.

🦔OpenAI CEO Sam Altman published a 13-page policy blueprint today proposing a new social contract for the AI era. The proposals include a public wealth fund giving every American a stake in AI-driven growth, taxes on automated labor, a four-day workweek pilot, and automatic safety net triggers that kick in when AI displacement hits preset thresholds. Altman told Axios superintelligence is close enough that capitalism as currently structured won't be adequate to handle what's coming.

My Take
I've spent months covering what this transition actually looks like for regular people. Oracle sending 30,000 termination emails at 6am. Microsoft freezing hiring in its core business. New graduates unable to find entry level jobs. Private credit cracking under software company debt. Companies using AI as cover to cut the people who built their products.

Now the CEO of the company driving a lot of this is publishing a policy paper saying the disruption is so serious it needs a New Deal scale response. He is not wrong about the scale. But OpenAI closed a $122 billion funding round just last week at an $852 billion valuation while losing billions annually. He is racing to build the thing, raising record amounts to do it faster, and asking government to protect the people his technology is displacing. 
The proposals themselves deserve serious debate because the alternative is nobody in power discussing this at all. But the people writing the blueprint are not the people losing the jobs, and that distance is important when you're deciding how much weight to give the solutions being proposed.

How a US–Iran Conflict Could Hit India’s Tech World
When tensions flare between the US and Iran, it might feel distant — but the ripple effects reach Indian tech workers and businesses faster than most people expect.
Global uncertainty makes companies nervous, and nervous companies cut budgets. That means IT projects get delayed, contracts dry up, and Indian firms that depend on US clients feel the squeeze first.
Then there’s the hardware problem. Semiconductors and tech components already travel through fragile supply chains. Add geopolitical instability to the mix, and prices climb — squeezing margins for companies that build or deploy technology.
Oil is the quieter troublemaker here. A spike in crude prices pushes up the cost of almost everything in India — transportation, power, goods. Inflation creeps in, and when businesses feel that pressure, hiring is usually the first thing they slow down.
That said, it’s not all bad news. Crisis tends to accelerate certain kinds of demand. Cybersecurity becomes a priority overnight. Companies lean harder into AI and automation to do more with leaner teams. For skilled professionals in those spaces, the same environment that creates uncertainty can also create opportunity.
The honest picture? It’s mixed. Some jobs will be harder to find. Others will be more in demand than ever. The key is knowing which side of that shift you’re on.

When you're building an AI Data Center, the single most powerful discipline you can adopt is this:
Stop planning forward. Start planning backward — from your Target Launch Date.
Here's the exact framework. 👇
🎯 STEP 1 — LOCK THE LAUNCH DATE. 
Your launch date is not a hope. It's not an estimate.
It is a stake in the ground — a commitment to clients, investors, and your team.Once it's fixed, your entire procurement, construction, compliance, and commissioning timeline has a backbone.
📅 STEP 2 — REVERSE ENGINEER THE CRITICAL PATH
Working backward from Launch Day:
🔴 T-0 → Launch Day
Facility fully live. AI workloads running. Clients onboarded. SLAs active
🟠 T-30 Days → Final Commissioning & Stress Testing
Full rack power-on. Network redundancy verification. Cooling validation under maximum load. Security systems sweep.
🟡 T-90 Days → Infrastructure Readiness
HV/LV power systems energized. Precision cooling operational. Structured cabling  fully active.
🟢 T-180 Days → Civil & MEP Completion
Data hall construction sealed. Generator bays commissioned. Fiber duct banks laid. Fire suppression systems certified.
🔵 T-270 Days → Equipment Procurement & On-Site Delivery
UPS systems, PDUs, CRAC/CRAH units, server racks, optical fiber, spine-leaf switching fabric — all ordered with lead-time buffers locked in.
⚠️ AI infra lead times globally run 16–24 weeks. 
🟣 T-365 Days → Regulatory & Compliance Clearances
Power utility agreements, environmental clearances, fire NOC, data sovereignty compliance, grid connectivity approvals — all in active motion.
⚫ T-450 Days → Land, Design & Financial Close
Site finalized. Masterplan approved. Architectural drawings frozen. Funding secured. EPC contracts signed.
⚠️ THE 5 INVISIBLE KILLERS
1. Power Delays
Utility coordination must begin on Day 1. Not Month 6. Grid capacity reservations are first-come, first-served.
2. Long-Lead Equipment
Transformers, diesel generators, and precision cooling towers carry 20–30 week global lead times. Order late — launch late.
3. Design Scope Creep
Every change after drawings are frozen costs 3× the time and 5× the budget. Freeze early. Hold the line.
4. Approval Bottlenecks
Regulatory NOCs operate on government time, not project time. Begin parallel processing from Month 1.
5. Commissioning Talent Scarcity
Certified data center engineers are rare. If you start hiring at T-60 days, you've already lost.

Forward planning says:
"Let's see how far we get by Q4."
Reverse planning says:
"Q4 is non-negotiable. Now let's engineer every single week backward until today."
This shift transforms your team from reactive builders to executors.
Every milestone becomes a countdown, not a calendar entry.
Every delay gets surfaced early — not discovered at handover.
Every vendor, every approval, every delivery slot is mapped to a consequence.
It's won by the team with the best timeline discipline. ⏱️
